concepts.name: Market-Creating Logic
concepts.definition: A way to move away from head-to-head competition by changing what buyers value and what the organization costs to deliver.

concepts.name: Value Innovation
concepts.definition: The simultaneous pursuit of buyer-value increase and cost reduction or cost redirection.

concepts.name: Chapter-Specific Framework
concepts.definition: Step 1 — Get Started: choose the right scope and assemble the team. Step 2 — Understand Where You Are Now: draw the as-is strategy canvas with brutal honesty. Step 3 — Imagine Where You Could Be: use the buyer utility map, three tiers of noncustomers, and the six paths to reconstruct market boundaries. Step 4 — Find the Path: the ERRC grid to design the to-be strategy canvas. Step 5 — Make the Move: rapid market testing at low cost, then scale.

concepts.name: Execution Discipline
concepts.definition: The managerial work that turns blue-ocean analysis into adoption, commitment, testing, and renewal.

arguments.claim: Blue-ocean work must change the value curve, not merely improve the old one
arguments.evidence: Competing on accepted factors tends to intensify red-ocean rivalry; the strategic task is to challenge factors, buyers, boundaries, utility, price, cost, and adoption barriers.
The book repeatedly uses visual maps, grids, and field inquiry because people commit more readily when assumptions, pain points, and alternatives are visible.

cases.name: Blue-ocean application case
cases.context: The chapter uses blue-ocean examples and tools to move from accepted industry logic to new demand.
cases.illustrates: How eliminating, reducing, raising, and creating factors reshapes buyer value.
cases.lesson: Copy the disciplined reconstruction pattern, not the surface case.

logicChains.description: Diagnose current red-ocean assumptions → expose buyer pain or noncustomers → reconstruct value factors → test viability and adoption → embed into execution and renewal.
logicChains.sourceHint: Synthesized from source chapter and index summary.

terminology.definition: The strategic profile showing how an offering invests across competition factors.
Eliminate, Reduce, Raise, Create — the four actions used to reconstruct value.
People outside current demand who may reveal the largest blue-ocean opportunity.

nuance: Blue ocean strategy is not mere differentiation; it joins differentiation with cost logic.
Tools fail when treated as one-off templates rather than decision disciplines.
